clinicalStem: A 16-hour-old male neonate born at 37 weeks gestation via spontaneous vaginal delivery is brought to the nursery for assessment. The mother had prolonged rupture of membranes for 22 hours and was febrile intrapartum (temperature 38.6°C). The neonate now appears lethargic, has a temperature of 38.2°C, respiratory rate of 72 breaths/min, and poor feeding. Capillary refill time is 3 seconds. Blood glucose is 2.1 mmol/L. What is the MOST appropriate immediate management?
options: Administer oral glucose gel and observe for 2 hours before deciding on antibiotics
Obtain blood culture and start intravenous ampicillin and gentamicin immediately
Perform a full septic workup including lumbar puncture before initiating any antibiotics
Start intravenous fluconazole empirically while awaiting culture results
Transfer to NICU for continuous monitoring without antibiotic therapy until cultures are positive
explanation: This neonate presents with early-onset sepsis risk factors (maternal fever, prolonged rupture of membranes >18 hours) and clinical signs of sepsis (lethargy, tachypnea, poor feeding, prolonged capillary refill, hypoglycemia). The standard empiric regimen for early-onset neonatal sepsis is intravenous ampicillin plus gentamicin, which covers Group B Streptococcus and gram-negative organisms including E. coli — the two most common causative pathogens. Blood culture should be obtained first but antibiotics must not be delayed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral glucose gel addresses hypoglycemia but does not treat the underlying sepsis; delaying antibiotics in a septic neonate is dangerous.
Correct: blood culture followed immediately by ampicillin and gentamicin is the evidence-based first-line empiric regimen for early-onset neonatal sepsis.
While lumbar puncture is part of a full septic workup, it should not delay antibiotic administration in an unstable neonate; LP can follow once the infant is stabilized.
Fluconazole covers fungal organisms; early-onset sepsis is almost exclusively bacterial (GBS, E. coli) and does not warrant empiric antifungal therapy.
Watchful waiting without antibiotics in a symptomatic neonate with multiple risk factors risks rapid deterioration and death from untreated sepsis.
clinicalPearl: In early-onset neonatal sepsis, never delay ampicillin + gentamicin waiting for a lumbar puncture — obtain blood culture, start antibiotics, then perform LP when the infant is stable.
keyClue: Maternal fever + prolonged ROM >18 hours + neonate with lethargy, tachypnea, prolonged capillary refill at <24 hours of life
commonExamTrap: Thinking a full septic workup (including LP) must be completed before starting antibiotics; in an unstable neonate, antibiotics come first.
missedConcept: Early-onset neonatal sepsis requires immediate empiric antibiotics without waiting for confirmatory cultures or full workup when the neonate is clinically unwell.
redFlagReason: Neonatal sepsis can progress to septic shock and death within hours; delayed antibiotic therapy dramatically worsens outcomes.

clinicalStem: A 9-day-old female neonate is brought by her parents because of 12 hours of high-pitched crying, poor feeding, and one episode of generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds. She was born at term via uncomplicated delivery and discharged home at 48 hours. On examination, temperature is 38.8°C, she is irritable, has a bulging anterior fontanelle, and nuchal rigidity. Blood glucose is 3.8 mmol/L. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Perform CT head to exclude raised intracranial pressure before any intervention
Administer rectal diazepam and monitor for further seizure activity
Obtain blood culture and CSF, then administer intravenous cefotaxime plus ampicillin immediately
Start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and arrange urgent outpatient neurology review
Administer intravenous dexamethasone alone while awaiting lumbar puncture results
explanation: This neonate has classical features of bacterial meningitis: fever, high-pitched cry, bulging fontanelle, nuchal rigidity, and a seizure. In late-onset neonatal meningitis (>7 days), empiric therapy should cover GBS, gram-negative enteric organisms, and Listeria monocytogenes. Cefotaxime plus ampicillin provides this broad coverage. Blood culture and CSF should be obtained immediately, but antibiotics must start without delay. CT prior to LP is not required in neonates as fontanelle examination is more reliable for raised ICP.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT head before any treatment wastes critical time; in neonates, a bulging fontanelle is a reliable sign and CT is not mandatory prior to LP or antibiotics in this age group.
Treating only the seizure without addressing the underlying meningitis leaves the primary infection untreated and risks rapid neurological deterioration.
Correct: blood culture + CSF followed immediately by cefotaxime + ampicillin is the appropriate empiric regimen for late-onset neonatal meningitis.
Oral antibiotics have inadequate CNS penetration and bioavailability in neonatal meningitis; outpatient management is completely inappropriate for this critically ill neonate.
Dexamethasone alone provides no antimicrobial cover; its use in neonatal meningitis is not well-established and it must never replace antibiotics.
clinicalPearl: Neonatal meningitis: bulging fontanelle + high-pitched cry + fever = empiric IV cefotaxime + ampicillin without delay; CT is not required before LP in neonates.
keyClue: 9-day-old with bulging anterior fontanelle, high-pitched cry, nuchal rigidity, and seizure
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT before LP wastes time; in neonates without focal neurology, proceed directly to LP and antibiotics.
missedConcept: Late-onset neonatal meningitis requires broader empiric coverage (including Listeria) with ampicillin plus cefotaxime; dexamethasone role is limited in neonates.
redFlagReason: Neonatal bacterial meningitis carries >20% mortality and significant neurological morbidity; antibiotic delay is directly linked to worse outcomes.

subtopic: Emergency management of SAM with complications
clinicalStem: An 18-month-old male child is brought to the emergency department by his grandmother in rural Nigeria. He is severely wasted with visible rib cage, bilateral pitting pedal oedema to the mid-calf, and a weight-for-height Z-score of −3.8. He is lethargic and unresponsive to verbal stimuli. Blood glucose on point-of-care testing is 1.8 mmol/L (32 mg/dL). He is not able to swallow due to decreased consciousness. Temperature is 35.6°C. What is the MOST appropriate immediate action?
options: Administer F-75 therapeutic milk via nasogastric tube at the standard rate
Give 10% glucose 5 mL/kg intravenously immediately, then start nasogastric feeds
Initiate oral rehydration with ReSoMal solution at 5 mL/kg every 30 minutes
Transfuse packed red blood cells empirically for presumed severe anaemia
Apply warm blankets and re-check blood glucose in one hour before intervening
explanation: This child has severe acute malnutrition (SAM) with a life-threatening complication: symptomatic hypoglycemia (BGL <3 mmol/L with decreased consciousness). WHO guidelines for SAM with hypoglycemia in a child who cannot swallow recommend immediate IV 10% glucose at 5 mL/kg. Once conscious, nasogastric F-75 should be started. Hypothermia must also be addressed simultaneously. Oral/NG feeds alone are inappropriate when the child cannot protect their airway.
wrongAnswerExplanations: F-75 via NGT is appropriate once the child is conscious; however, the immediate priority in an unresponsive hypoglycemic child is IV glucose to rapidly correct the life-threatening blood sugar.
Correct: IV 10% glucose 5 mL/kg is the WHO-recommended immediate treatment for symptomatic hypoglycemia in SAM when the child cannot swallow.
ReSoMal is for rehydration in SAM with diarrhoea; it does not correct acute hypoglycemia and is contraindicated in an unresponsive child unable to swallow.

There is no evidence of severe anaemia here; empiric blood transfusion in SAM carries risk of fluid overload and is not indicated without clear evidence of haemoglobin <4 g/dL or severe respiratory distress.
Delaying treatment in symptomatic hypoglycemia risks permanent neurological damage or death; immediate correction is mandatory.
clinicalPearl: In SAM with symptomatic hypoglycemia and decreased consciousness, give IV 10% glucose 5 mL/kg immediately — do not wait, and do not attempt oral/NGT feeds until the child is conscious.
keyClue: SAM + unresponsive child + BGL 1.8 mmol/L (cannot swallow)
commonExamTrap: Defaulting to NGT F-75 feeds without recognizing that an unresponsive child cannot safely receive NGT feeds and needs IV glucose first.
missedConcept: SAM with hypoglycemia and impaired consciousness is a medical emergency requiring IV glucose correction before enteral feeding.
redFlagReason: Untreated symptomatic hypoglycemia in SAM causes irreversible brain damage and death within minutes to hours.

clinicalStem: A 10-month-old female infant is brought for routine follow-up. Her mother reports she has been exclusively breastfed since birth and no solid foods have been introduced. She appears pale. Full blood count shows: Hb 8.6 g/dL, MCV 62 fL, MCH 19 pg, reticulocyte count mildly elevated. Serum ferritin is 4 ng/mL. She is otherwise well, gaining weight, and has no bleeding history. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Arrange bone marrow biopsy to exclude aplastic anaemia before starting treatment
Start oral elemental iron at 3–6 mg/kg/day and introduce iron-rich complementary foods
Transfuse packed red blood cells given haemoglobin below 9 g/dL
Prescribe folic acid supplementation and recheck FBC in 3 months
Advise continuation of exclusive breastfeeding as breast milk has superior iron absorption
explanation: This infant has classic iron deficiency anaemia (IDA): microcytic hypochromic anaemia, low ferritin, exclusive breastfeeding beyond 6 months without introduction of complementary foods (breast milk alone is insufficient in iron after 6 months). Treatment is oral elemental iron 3–6 mg/kg/day, and complementary iron-rich foods should be introduced immediately. Blood transfusion is not indicated for asymptomatic moderate anaemia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bone marrow biopsy is not indicated for straightforward microcytic hypochromic anaemia with low ferritin in this age group; the diagnosis of IDA is clinically clear.
Correct: oral elemental iron 3–6 mg/kg/day plus introduction of iron-rich complementary foods is the standard treatment for IDA in infancy.
Packed red cell transfusion is reserved for Hb <7 g/dL with haemodynamic compromise or Hb <8 g/dL with severe symptoms; this infant is asymptomatic and clinically stable.
Folic acid treats macrocytic/megaloblastic anaemia; this infant has microcytic, hypochromic anaemia with low ferritin consistent with iron deficiency, not folate deficiency.

While breast milk iron is highly bioavailable, its concentration is insufficient to meet the iron requirements of infants beyond 6 months; exclusive breastfeeding after 6 months without solid foods causes IDA.
clinicalPearl: Exclusively breastfed infants need iron-rich complementary foods introduced by 6 months — breast milk alone cannot prevent iron deficiency anaemia after this age.
keyClue: 10-month-old exclusively breastfed with no solids + microcytic hypochromic anaemia + low ferritin
commonExamTrap: Recommending packed red cell transfusion for moderate asymptomatic IDA, or continuing exclusive breastfeeding thinking it provides adequate iron.
missedConcept: Breast milk iron content is low and decreases further over time; exclusive breastfeeding beyond 6 months without complementary foods is a major risk factor for IDA.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. He developed sudden-onset high fever (39.8°C), severe sore throat, and progressive difficulty swallowing over 3 hours. He is now sitting forward in a tripod position, drooling, and appears anxious. His voice is muffled. Oxygen saturation is 93% on room air. On examination you note suprasternal and intercostal recession. He is fully vaccinated per the national schedule but received Hib vaccine only at 6 months due to parental hesitation. What is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT immediate action?
options: Perform throat examination with a tongue depressor to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention
Obtain a lateral neck X-ray in the radiology department immediately
Summon senior anaesthetist/ENT surgeon and secure the airway in a controlled setting
Administer nebulised adrenaline and oral dexamethasone, then reassess in 30 minutes
Start intravenous co-amoxiclav and observe in the emergency bay with continuous monitoring
explanation: This child has classic acute epiglottitis: sudden high fever, severe sore throat, drooling, muffled voice, tripod posture, stridor, and hypoxia. The immediate priority is airway security in a controlled environment with the most experienced airway personnel (senior anaesthetist and ENT). Any unnecessary manipulation — throat examination, lying the child flat for X-ray, or upsetting the child — can cause complete airway obstruction. Antibiotics and imaging follow only after the airway is secured.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Throat examination with a tongue depressor in suspected epiglottitis can precipitate complete laryngospasm and airway obstruction — this is absolutely contraindicated.
Sending a child with impending airway obstruction to radiology unaccompanied or lying flat is dangerous; lateral neck X-ray is not required and must not delay airway management.
Correct: the top priority is calling senior anaesthesia and ENT to secure the airway in theatre/ICU under controlled conditions before complete obstruction occurs.
Nebulised adrenaline is used for croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), not epiglottitis; a 30-minute reassessment delay in acute epiglottitis risks fatal airway loss.
While antibiotics are indicated, starting them in the ED before securing the airway is secondary; upsetting the child during IV cannulation can trigger complete obstruction.
clinicalPearl: In suspected acute epiglottitis — drooling, tripod posture, muffled voice, high fever — do NOT examine the throat. Call anaesthetics + ENT immediately to secure the airway in a controlled environment.
keyClue: Tripod posture + drooling + muffled voice + sudden high fever + SpO2 93% + suprasternal recession
commonExamTrap: Performing throat examination or ordering a lateral neck X-ray before securing the airway; in epiglottitis, these interventions can be fatal.
missedConcept: Acute epiglottitis is an airway emergency — secure the airway first, everything else second; do not disturb the child until in a controlled setting.
redFlagReason: Acute epiglottitis can cause complete airway obstruction and asphyxiation within minutes; any delay or inappropriate manipulation is potentially fatal.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old female presents with a 2-day history of right ear pain, fever of 38.5°C, and increased irritability. She has had three episodes of acute otitis media in the past 12 months. On otoscopy, the right tympanic membrane is erythematous, bulging, and has no light reflex. There is no mastoid tenderness. She has no known drug allergies. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Prescribe a 5-day course of oral amoxicillin and advise analgesics
Reassure and prescribe analgesics alone with follow-up in 48 hours; antibiotics only if not improving
Arrange urgent ENT referral for myringotomy
Prescribe oral azithromycin for 3 days as first-line therapy
Order a CT of the temporal bone to assess mastoid involvement
explanation: This 2-year-old has acute otitis media (AOM) with a bulging tympanic membrane (which confirms bacterial AOM) and is under 2 years of age with recurrent AOM (3 episodes in 12 months). Current guidelines recommend antibiotic treatment without a watchful waiting period for children <2 years with confirmed AOM (bulging TM) and for those with recurrent AOM. Amoxicillin remains first-line. A 5-day course is appropriate for non-severe cases in this age group (some guidelines use 10 days for children <2).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: amoxicillin is first-line for AOM, and antibiotics are indicated immediately in children <2 years with a bulging TM, without watchful waiting.
Watchful waiting is acceptable for children ≥2 years with mild, unilateral AOM; it is not appropriate for children <2 years with bilateral or severe AOM, or a bulging TM with recurrent episodes.
Myringotomy is not indicated for uncomplicated AOM; it is reserved for recurrent AOM requiring tympanostomy tubes or AOM with complications such as mastoiditis.
Azithromycin is not first-line for AOM; it is reserved for patients with penicillin allergy. Amoxicillin provides superior coverage for S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae.
CT temporal bone is indicated only if mastoiditis is suspected (mastoid tenderness, post-auricular swelling, pinna displaced forward); this child has no such features.
clinicalPearl: In children <2 years with a bulging tympanic membrane, do not observe — treat immediately with amoxicillin; watchful waiting is only for older children with mild unilateral AOM.
keyClue: Age <2 years + bulging erythematous tympanic membrane + recurrent AOM
commonExamTrap: Applying watchful waiting strategy to a child <2 years with a bulging TM; guidelines mandate immediate antibiotic treatment in this group.
missedConcept: A bulging tympanic membrane indicates bacterial AOM and mandates antibiotic treatment, regardless of the watchful waiting debate.

clinicalStem: A 22-month-old male is brought to the emergency department at 2 AM. His parents report a 6-hour history of a barking cough, hoarse voice, and noisy breathing. He has a temperature of 37.9°C. On examination, he has inspiratory stridor at rest, moderate suprasternal and intercostal retractions, but maintains oxygen saturation of 97% on room air. He is alert and interacts with his parents. He has no drooling and no muffled voice. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Prepare for immediate intubation in the operating theatre
Administer a single dose of oral dexamethasone (0.15–0.6 mg/kg) and consider nebulised adrenaline
Give nebulised salbutamol and oral prednisolone for 3 days
Administer intravenous ceftriaxone and arrange ENT review for possible epiglottitis
Discharge home with steam inhalation advice and follow-up with GP in 48 hours
explanation: This child has moderate croup (stridor at rest with retractions, SpO2 preserved, alert). Oral or IM dexamethasone (0.15–0.6 mg/kg) is the cornerstone of croup management as it reduces airway oedema and hospital admission rates. Nebulised adrenaline (epinephrine) is added for moderate-severe croup with significant respiratory distress. The presentation is clearly croup — barking cough, hoarse voice, no drooling, no muffled voice — distinguishing it from epiglottitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Immediate intubation is reserved for severe croup with impending respiratory failure (severe retractions, cyanosis, exhaustion, altered consciousness); this child has moderate croup and is alert with good SpO2.
Correct: dexamethasone is the mainstay treatment for croup; nebulised adrenaline is added for moderate-to-severe cases with stridor at rest and retractions.
Salbutamol has no role in croup — croup is caused by subglottic oedema, not bronchospasm. Prednisolone is less preferred than dexamethasone due to less evidence and shorter duration of action.
This is classic croup, not epiglottitis; the barking cough, gradual onset, hoarse voice, and absence of drooling or muffled voice make epiglottitis very unlikely. IV antibiotics are not indicated.
Stridor at rest with retractions represents moderate croup and requires active treatment; discharge with steam inhalation is insufficient and potentially dangerous at this severity.
clinicalPearl: Stridor at rest = moderate croup = give dexamethasone + nebulised adrenaline; do not discharge with stridor at rest until the child has been observed for at least 2–4 hours after treatment.
keyClue: Barking cough + hoarse voice + inspiratory stridor AT REST + retractions + SpO2 97% + alert
commonExamTrap: Giving salbutamol (used for bronchospasm/asthma) or confusing this with epiglottitis — the barking cough and hoarse voice without drooling distinguish croup.
missedConcept: Croup management centres on steroids (dexamethasone) ± nebulised adrenaline; salbutamol and antibiotics play no role.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old unvaccinated male from a community with low immunization coverage presents with a 4-day history of high fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis, followed 2 days later by a maculopapular rash that started on the face and spread downward to the trunk and limbs. On examination he has Koplik spots on the buccal mucosa. His respiratory rate is 48 breaths/min and SpO2 is 91% on room air. Auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Start antiviral therapy with oral ribavirin and admit to respiratory isolation
Administer high-dose vitamin A, supplemental oxygen, and treat concurrent bacterial pneumonia with antibiotics
Discharge home with oral paracetamol and return precautions; measles is self-limiting
Give intravenous immunoglobulin and refer to infectious disease for antiviral options
Start prophylactic antiretrovirals given the possibility of secondary HIV in an unvaccinated child
explanation: This child has measles complicated by pneumonia (the most common fatal complication of measles). WHO recommends high-dose vitamin A supplementation for all children with measles as it significantly reduces mortality and severity — given once daily for 2 days. Secondary bacterial pneumonia (most commonly Streptococcus pneumoniae, H. influenzae) is a major cause of measles-related deaths and requires empiric antibiotics. Supplemental oxygen addresses hypoxia. Respiratory isolation should also be implemented.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ribavirin is not standard treatment for measles; it has no established role in routine measles management. Measles is caused by the measles virus (paramyxovirus) for which ribavirin has no approved indication.
Correct: high-dose vitamin A reduces measles mortality and severity; bacterial pneumonia requires antibiotics; and oxygen corrects hypoxia — this is the complete evidence-based management bundle.
Discharging a child with measles complicated by pneumonia (SpO2 91%) is dangerous; this child needs hospital admission, oxygen, vitamin A, and antibiotics.
IV immunoglobulin is used for post-exposure prophylaxis in susceptible high-risk individuals, not for treatment of established complicated measles.
There is no indication for antiretrovirals here; unvaccinated status does not imply HIV infection, and antiretrovirals do not treat measles.
clinicalPearl: All children with measles should receive high-dose vitamin A — it reduces mortality by up to 50%. In complicated measles with pneumonia, add empiric antibiotics and oxygen.
keyClue: Koplik spots + cephalocaudal maculopapular rash + unvaccinated + bilateral crackles + SpO2 91%
commonExamTrap: Forgetting vitamin A supplementation in measles management, or treating as uncomplicated measles when there is evidence of pneumonia.
missedConcept: Vitamin A deficiency worsens measles outcomes; WHO mandates vitamin A supplementation for all children with measles, regardless of nutritional status.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old male living in a dengue-endemic region presents with a 5-day history of high fever, severe headache, and myalgia. Today he developed sudden defervescence, abdominal pain, and vomiting. On examination: BP 85/60 mmHg (baseline >100/70), pulse 128 bpm and weak, cold and clammy extremities, capillary refill 4 seconds. Platelet count is 28,000/µL. Haematocrit is 52% (baseline 38%). Which of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate action?
options: Transfuse 2 units of platelet concentrate immediately given platelet count below 50,000/µL
Administer IV crystalloid (normal saline or Ringer's lactate) bolus at 10–20 mL/kg over 15–30 minutes
Start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for bacterial septic shock
Administer aspirin for its antiplatelet and antipyretic effects in dengue
Arrange urgent CT abdomen to exclude dengue haemorrhagic complications before fluid resuscitation
explanation: This child is in dengue shock syndrome (DSS): compensated shock with tachycardia, hypotension, cold extremities, prolonged CRT, and rising haematocrit (>20% rise from baseline, indicating plasma leakage). Fluid resuscitation with IV crystalloid (normal saline or Ringer's lactate) at 10–20 mL/kg over 15–30 minutes is the WHO-recommended immediate management for DSS. Platelet transfusion is reserved for active bleeding with severe thrombocytopenia, not prophylactically. Aspirin is absolutely contraindicated in dengue.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Platelet transfusion is not indicated prophylactically in dengue, even with low platelet counts; it is only given for active significant bleeding. The priority here is volume resuscitation for shock.
Correct: immediate IV crystalloid bolus 10–20 mL/kg over 15–30 minutes is the first-line treatment for dengue shock syndrome to restore circulating volume.
This is dengue shock (viral), not bacterial sepsis; empiric antibiotics do not address the underlying pathophysiology of plasma leakage and are not indicated as first-line management.
Aspirin is absolutely contraindicated in dengue fever as it increases the risk of bleeding and Reye syndrome; only paracetamol should be used for fever in dengue.
CT abdomen would delay life-saving fluid resuscitation; in dengue shock, immediate fluid therapy takes absolute priority over imaging.
clinicalPearl: In dengue shock syndrome, the haematocrit rise >20% from baseline signals plasma leakage — treat with IV crystalloid bolus immediately. Aspirin is absolutely contraindicated in dengue.
keyClue: Dengue-endemic region + day 5 defervescence + hypotension + rising haematocrit >20% + cold clammy extremities = dengue shock syndrome
commonExamTrap: Transfusing platelets prophylactically in dengue thrombocytopenia or using aspirin for fever/antiplatelet effect — both are dangerous in dengue.
missedConcept: Dengue shock is caused by plasma leakage (haemoconcentration), not haemorrhage — treat with fluid resuscitation, not platelet transfusion.
redFlagReason: Dengue shock syndrome (DSS) carries significant mortality without prompt fluid resuscitation; delay leads to irreversible circulatory collapse.

clinicalStem: A 3-day-old female neonate is brought to the clinic with profuse purulent bilateral eye discharge. The eyelids are markedly swollen and erythematous. Her mother had untreated gonorrhoea during pregnancy, confirmed on antenatal swab. Gram stain of the eye discharge shows intracellular gram-negative diplococci. The neonate is otherwise well with stable vital signs. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Topical tetracycline eye ointment applied four times daily for 7 days
Topical chloramphenicol eye drops and oral erythromycin for 14 days
Single dose of intravenous or intramuscular ceftriaxone 25–50 mg/kg plus eye irrigation with normal saline
Oral amoxicillin for 5 days with saline eye irrigation
Topical silver nitrate drops once only and discharge home with follow-up in 48 hours
explanation: Neonatal gonococcal ophthalmia (GON) is a medical emergency requiring systemic treatment — topical therapy alone is insufficient. WHO and CDC guidelines recommend a single dose of IV or IM ceftriaxone 25–50 mg/kg (maximum 125 mg) plus frequent eye irrigation with normal saline. Ceftriaxone covers penicillinase-producing N. gonorrhoeae and achieves adequate ocular and systemic levels. Untreated GON can cause corneal perforation and permanent blindness within hours.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical tetracycline alone is completely inadequate for gonococcal ophthalmia; systemic antibiotic therapy is mandatory as topical agents cannot achieve sufficient tissue levels to clear the infection.
Chloramphenicol and oral erythromycin do not provide adequate coverage for N. gonorrhoeae; erythromycin is used for chlamydial ophthalmia, not gonococcal.
Correct: single-dose IM/IV ceftriaxone plus saline eye irrigation is the WHO/CDC-recommended treatment for neonatal gonococcal ophthalmia.
Amoxicillin does not reliably cover penicillinase-producing N. gonorrhoeae strains and is not an appropriate treatment for confirmed neonatal gonococcal infection.
Topical silver nitrate is used only for prophylaxis at birth, not treatment; it has no role once active gonococcal ophthalmia is established.
clinicalPearl: Neonatal gonococcal ophthalmia requires systemic ceftriaxone (single IM/IV dose) — topical antibiotics alone are never adequate and risk corneal blindness.
keyClue: 3-day-old + gram-negative intracellular diplococci + profuse purulent discharge + maternal gonorrhoea history
commonExamTrap: Using topical antibiotics alone or prescribing erythromycin (which is for chlamydial, not gonococcal, neonatal conjunctivitis).
missedConcept: Gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum is a systemic infection requiring IV/IM ceftriaxone; chlamydial conjunctivitis is treated with oral erythromycin — these two conditions require different antibiotics.
redFlagReason: Untreated neonatal gonococcal ophthalmia causes corneal ulceration and perforation within 24–48 hours, resulting in permanent blindness.

clinicalStem: A 14-month-old male toddler is brought by his mother with bowing of both legs noticed since he started walking at 12 months. He was exclusively breastfed for 12 months with no supplementation and lives in a region with low sunlight exposure. On examination he has frontal bossing, widened wrists and ankles, and Harrison's sulcus. His weight and height are on the 25th centile. X-ray of the wrists shows cupping and fraying of the metaphyses. Serum calcium is 1.9 mmol/L, phosphate 0.7 mmol/L, ALP 890 IU/L (markedly elevated). What is the MOST appropriate initial treatment?
options: High-dose oral calcium supplementation alone for 3 months
Oral vitamin D (cholecalciferol) supplementation at therapeutic doses plus adequate dietary calcium intake
Intravenous pamidronate infusion for severe metabolic bone disease
Surgical correction of lower limb bowing immediately
Topical vitamin D cream applied to skin and increased outdoor sunlight exposure alone
explanation: This child has nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets: bowing legs, frontal bossing, widened wrists (cupping/fraying on X-ray), hypocalcaemia, hypophosphataemia, and markedly elevated ALP, with clear risk factors (exclusively breastfed, low sunlight). First-line treatment is oral vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) at therapeutic stoss doses (e.g., 2000–6000 IU/day for 3 months) or high-dose stoss therapy, combined with adequate calcium intake. Bone deformities often resolve with medical treatment alone.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Calcium alone without vitamin D cannot correct nutritional rickets; the primary deficiency is vitamin D, which is required for calcium absorption from the gut.
Correct: oral vitamin D at therapeutic doses plus adequate dietary calcium is the evidence-based first-line treatment for nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets.
IV bisphosphonates (pamidronate) are used for osteogenesis imperfecta and osteoporosis, not nutritional rickets; they are not indicated and would be harmful here.

Surgical correction of bowing is not indicated as a first step — most bone deformities in nutritional rickets remodel with vitamin D and calcium repletion; surgery is reserved for persistent deformity after biochemical correction.
Topical vitamin D has negligible systemic bioavailability and is not an effective treatment; sunlight alone is insufficient for established rickets requiring therapeutic vitamin D doses.
clinicalPearl: Nutritional rickets is treated with oral vitamin D3 at therapeutic doses plus adequate calcium — most bowing deformities remodel without surgery once biochemical deficiency is corrected.
keyClue: Exclusively breastfed + no supplementation + low sunlight + bowing + cupping/fraying on X-ray + low calcium/phosphate + high ALP
commonExamTrap: Recommending immediate surgery for the bowed legs or giving calcium alone without vitamin D.
missedConcept: Nutritional rickets is a deficiency of vitamin D (not calcium primarily); calcium supplementation without vitamin D is insufficient as vitamin D is essential for gut calcium absorption.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old male with nephrotic syndrome on oral prednisolone (2 mg/kg/day for 6 weeks) develops a vesicular pruritic rash starting on the trunk and spreading to the face and limbs. New lesions continue to appear over 48 hours. He has 400 vesicular and crusted lesions, temperature 39.2°C, and is lethargic. He had no prior varicella vaccination. His mother reports he was exposed to a confirmed varicella case at school 14 days ago. Chest X-ray shows bilateral infiltrates consistent with varicella pneumonitis. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Continue oral prednisolone unchanged, start oral aciclovir, and manage as outpatient
Stop prednisolone abruptly, administer varicella-zoster immune globulin (VZIG), and discharge home
Admit to hospital, reduce/stop prednisolone cautiously (adrenal suppression risk), and start intravenous aciclovir
Reassure that varicella is usually self-limiting; prescribe antihistamines for itch and follow up in 5 days
Administer oral valaciclovir and parenteral hydrocortisone stress dosing then discharge
explanation: This immunocompromised child (on high-dose systemic corticosteroids) has severe varicella complicated by pneumonitis — a life-threatening complication. IV aciclovir is indicated for severe or complicated varicella in immunocompromised patients; oral aciclovir has inadequate bioavailability for severe disease. Prednisolone must be reduced/stopped cautiously (abrupt discontinuation after prolonged use risks adrenal crisis). Hospital admission for monitoring, respiratory support, and IV antiviral therapy is mandatory.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Continuing high-dose prednisolone without dose reduction will further impair the immune response to varicella; oral aciclovir has insufficient bioavailability in severe/complicated disease; outpatient management is unsafe with pneumonitis.
Abrupt prednisolone discontinuation risks adrenal crisis after 6 weeks of high-dose steroids; VZIG is used for post-exposure prophylaxis, not treatment of established infection.
Correct: hospital admission + cautious prednisolone reduction + IV aciclovir is the appropriate management for severe varicella with pneumonitis in an immunocompromised child.
Varicella in an immunocompromised child with pneumonitis is a medical emergency with significant mortality; antihistamines alone and delayed follow-up are dangerous and inappropriate.
Oral valaciclovir does not achieve adequate plasma levels for severe/complicated varicella; the child requires IV aciclovir and hospital-level care, not discharge.
clinicalPearl: Immunocompromised children with varicella and systemic complications (pneumonitis, encephalitis, hepatitis) need hospital admission and IV aciclovir — oral antivirals are insufficient for severe disease.
keyClue: Immunocompromised (nephrotic syndrome on high-dose steroids) + varicella + bilateral pulmonary infiltrates = severe complicated varicella requiring IV aciclovir
commonExamTrap: Using oral aciclovir (insufficient bioavailability in severe disease) or abruptly stopping corticosteroids (risking adrenal crisis after 6 weeks of therapy).
missedConcept: IV aciclovir is required for complicated varicella in immunocompromised patients; prednisolone must be tapered carefully to avoid adrenal insufficiency.
redFlagReason: Varicella pneumonitis in an immunocompromised child carries significant mortality; delayed IV antiviral therapy and uncontrolled immunosuppression are life-threatening.
